Ynu ask, first, whethet tne individuals named in

it is our conclusmn that no disqualifie

Thus, one of the factors reguired
to he present in this case before disqualification is triggered
is that Bank of America or wells rargn Bank be a source of
income to the employees involved.
"Income” is defined broad” in Section 82030 of
the Act to mean "income of. any nature from any source,"
including, among other things; any “advance, payment,
interest."
“Payment,"
in turn,
is defined as "a payment,
distribution. transfer, loan, advance, deposit, gift or
other rendering of money, property, services or anything
else of value, whether tangible or intangible" (Section
52044).
Excluded {tom the definition of income is "[ilnterest,
dividends or premiums on a time or demand deposit in a financial
institution" (Section aznmwuni.
Applying those definitions to the situation you
Pose, it is our conclusion that an individual does not receive
income simply by maintaining a checking or savings account
at a bank
Fonds deposited in a bank remain the property of
the depositer and their vithdrawal cannnt fairly be said to
tie a payment to the holder of the account.
Moreover, any
interest the depositer recoives is speeiticallv exempted
from the definition of income by Section EZD3U(D](7L
We also do not believe that the use of a bank
credit card ordinarily creates reportable income.
while a
literal reading of the Act's definitions of "income' and
“payment" may he said to cover the advancing of credit, we
have concluded that the policy chieeeivee oi the not are not

The Community Redevelopment Commission of the Cit] of Pomona
has requested me to ask for a formal opinion as to whether
that Commission must disclose as e "designated employee" under
the Conflict of interest Code currently in preparation by my
office for city agencies,
Heretotnre.
I have requested of your staff an informal opinion.
a copy of which is attached hereto
It was, and is my opinion
that this Commission. Earned under Section 33201, health and
Safety Code, should not be required to disclose as it makes no
final governmental decision.
The scope of its duties is mainly
advisory to the City Council of Pomona, which also site as the
Governing Body of the Redeveldnnent Agency as the City of
Pomona.
Its reoomendations are not "ruther stamped" by the
Coverning Body, hut on many occasions are rejected or modified
The closest that the Commission comes to making a final govern-
mental decision is when it prepares a redevelopment plan for a
orojeet area, prior to its creation
The final authority whether
to adopt the plan and the project area rests with the city council,
and no Commission notion relatiny to redevelopment can he made
Without the prior ilnal approval of the governing body or the
City Council

sections.question: 1) Whether certain designated employees at the Department of Corporations with financial relationships to Bank of America and Wells Fargo Bank must disqualify themselves from reviewing a Bank of America application to issue mortgage pool Certificates; 2) Whether such employees must report various transactions with these banks (credit card purchases, account withdrawals, check writing, etc.)
sections.conclusion: No disqualification is required. Maintaining checking/savings accounts and using standard credit cards do not constitute reportable income. Employees with loans from Wells Fargo Bank do have a reporting obligation, but this does not trigger disqualification under the exception in Section 87103. Routine banking transactions (account withdrawals, check writing, and standard credit card purchases) create no reporting obligation; special credit terms outside ordinary business practice would trigger reporting requirements.
sections.question_synthetic: The Department of Corporations asked whether employees with existing financial relationships to Bank of America and Wells Fargo Bank must disqualify themselves from reviewing a Bank of America mortgage pool certificate application, and whether they must report routine banking transactions.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that no disqualification was required based on standard banking relationships, though employees with outstanding loans must report them. Routine banking transactions and standard credit card use do not create reporting obligations under the Political Reform Act.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains OCR errors affecting readability in places. Primary advice letter regarding Opinion Request No. 77-019 is clearly identifiable. Document also includes an unrelated incoming request letter from City of Pomona dated April 18, 1977 requesting formal opinion on Community Redevelopment Commission disclosure requirements; only the first letter (77-019) appears to be the main FPPC response document described in the parsing notes.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1) Whether certain designated employees at the Department of Corporations with financial relationships to Bank of America and Wells Fargo Bank must disqualify themselves from reviewing a Bank of America application to issue mortgage pool Certificates; 2) Whether such employees must report various transactions with these banks (credit card purchases, account withdrawals, check writing, etc.)

CONCLUSION: No disqualification is required. Maintaining checking/savings accounts and using standard credit cards do not constitute reportable income. Employees with loans from Wells Fargo Bank do have a reporting obligation, but this does not trigger disqualification under the exception in Section 87103. Routine banking transactions (account withdrawals, check writing, and standard credit card purchases) create no reporting obligation; special credit terms outside ordinary business practice would trigger reporting requirements.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to Department of Corporations' inquiry regarding conflict of interest disqualification and reporting obligations for employees reviewing a Bank of America application where those employees have financial relationships with the banks involved. The Commission concluded no disqualification was needed and provided guidance on what constitutes reportable income and transactions.
